title: Kortex The $3 Million Dollar Mistake
author: Dan Koe

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Kortex: The $3 Million Dollar Mistake - YouTube (00:00) Cortex, the $3 million mistake. One of the things I started thinking about is like what are the problems with Cortex? Like we can't make any step forward because we're just like marching in place. Then the comments coming in and everything else. It's like what are we doing? Let's set these goals. (00:17) Let's make them really aggressive and see what happens. And we did that. And we saw that the whole car fell apart. We need desktop. We need light mode. Light mode. Yes. cuz we could like that's how that's how slow we were going is like it was difficult to get light mode of all things and so we threw away the one and only advantage that we really had as a startup which is moving fast. We haven't tried. (00:40) Why don't we just try? Like surely if we do those things we can rebuild the entirety of Cortex and more in much shorter time. Literally 3 3 weeks into being in Japan that hypothesis turned out to be true. In terms of like rebranding from Cortex to Eden, what comes to mind for you? It's like a search engine for your memories. (01:04) I just went in and searched pink sweater and it resurfaced every single frame where someone was wearing a pink sweater at the exact timestamp. This is insane. I haven't seen this before. The second that you think you know what you're doing, you're kind of [ __ ] And it's like, I don't know how to freaking manage a team, dude. I don't know the first thing that goes into this. (01:20) So, like me going into it, as long as I saw some form of progress, I'm like, ah yeah, we're actually doing something. success is just like a a series of perhaps well-calulated risks, but like a series of big risks. Because as humans, we're very prone to remaking the same mistakes over and over again. Well, yeah, we're here to talk about why Cortex failed. Yeah. $3 million mistake. That's the title of this video. (01:45) Very attention grabbing and polarizing, but yeah. Yeah. Uh on the topic of cortex being a mistake um I don't think it was actually a mistake but I want to hear like your take on that right you I mean you know my philosophy mistakes you have to make them in order to actually improve. Yeah. (02:06) So do you see it as a mistake? I I I see it as a series of necessary mistakes for us to get where we need to go. Um, and fundamentally like I don't think the idea of building Cortex, the second brain app, was a bad one, but there were a lot of mistakes that we made as like firsttime founders that went into Cortex that uh definitely can be categorized as mistakes. Yeah, if you follow me, you know I talked for about Cortex non-stop for however many months or even years and then just stopped all of a sudden. (02:38) And then people are like, "Oh, what's happening to Cortex? Where's Cortex?" Uh, a lot of things happened and we decided to pivot and build a new app, Eden. So, that sparks a ton of questions of like, okay, why didn't you stay building Cortex? Why are you doing Eden? Why did you rebrand? The first thing there is like where we stopped with Cortex wasn't our vision for it. (03:02) We kind of just stopped there. Yeah. Because we were forced to for the reasons we're going to talk about. Yeah, but to frame even further and to say this early on in the video, Cortex users, you probably already know what's happening. You've gotten emails about it. (03:20) If you don't know what's happening, starting Monday, the week right before the Monday right before Black Friday, we're going to start rolling out Eden to you. So, expect an email on that. Log into the app if you don't get an email cuz you'll get a banner in the app to transfer everything over if you want to. But for people who have not used Cortex, have been hearing about Eden, are very excited about it, Black Friday weekend from Friday to Monday, we're going to try and open it to the public and see if things are okay. Cortex users, it's not going to be up to par like having desktop app and mobile and everything else with Cortex. So, just please keep an eye on all the information. But what was Cortex? Let's start at the beginning. Like the very beginning. (04:04) Yeah, the very beginning. How did we meet? Yeah, that's kind of how we met. My conditioning from a very young age (09:36) because of my parents was that, you know, anything's possible. You can do whatever you want. It was compartmentalized based on tech stack. And that was fundamentally the wrong approach because years into a year and or or two into doing that, we realized that way of doing things at an early stage startup is so slow. like you're structuring your team the way big tech companies structure their teams for the wrong reasons. (13:34) It makes much more sense for every single person on the team to be laser focused on product to be laser focused on like what is it that we're actually trying to build and can we get product market fit and what that translates to is everybody should be full stack.
